title: Buffer vs Window vs Tab
subtitle: Three concepts most editors collapse into one. Vim keeps them separate.
summary: A buffer is a file in memory. A window is a viewport onto a buffer. A tab is a layout of windows. Most editors call all three 'tabs' — that's why Vim's model feels strange at first.

blocks.text: Most editors give you *one* concept — call it a tab, a panel, whatever — and that one thing represents "a file you're editing." Vim breaks it into three. Once you see the split, the rest of Vim's window model snaps into place.

blocks.title: The Three Layers
blocks.text:
**Buffer** — a *file in memory*. You open a file, Vim loads its contents into a buffer. The buffer is the editable text. There's no UI for it directly; you see a buffer through a window.

**Window** — a *viewport* onto a buffer. The split layout you see is windows. One buffer can be shown in many windows; one window shows one buffer at a time.

**Tab** — a *layout of windows*. A way to save and switch between window arrangements.

Concrete picture: open three files (three buffers). Split the screen into two windows; each window shows a buffer. Open a tab; lay out four windows there showing yet other buffers. Total: 7 buffers, 6 windows, 2 tabs. Closing a window doesn't unload its buffer — the buffer is still in memory, just not currently visible.

blocks.title: Why This Matters
blocks.text:
Once you treat *buffers* as your unit of work and *windows*/*tabs* as transient layout decisions:

  • You stop trying to "close the tab" and start "closing the window, the file's still there."
  • You can have 50 buffers loaded with no UI clutter — they're invisible until you `:b` to one.
  • You arrange splits the way that helps your *current task*, not your *file inventory*.
  • You stop fighting Vim's defaults.

Your mental model: buffers are the data; windows are the UI; tabs are the saved UI layouts. Most professional Vim users live in 1–3 windows and zero tabs, switching between many buffers.

blocks.title: Three nested containers, not three competing concepts
blocks.text: The data model is strictly nested: tabs contain windows, windows show buffers, buffers hold lines. A tab is a window-layout container; it doesn't own text. A window is a viewport into a buffer; it doesn't own text either. Only the buffer owns text. This explains every navigation command: `:b`-prefixed jumps switch buffer in the current window; {key:Ctrl-W} jumps switch window in the current tab; {key:g}{key:t} jumps switch tab.

blocks.title: Buffer state — the flags every buffer carries
blocks.text: A buffer isn't just a blob of text — it carries a small state record. The five fields you'll meet most often in error messages and `:set` output:

rows: Buffer has unsaved changes. Vim refuses to abandon it without `!` or `:hide`.
Whether the user is allowed to edit. Off = every edit beeps with `E21`.
Soft block — you *can* override with `:w!`, unlike `'modifiable'`.
Whether `:ls` and buffer-cycling commands include it.
Kind of buffer. Most are normal (empty string); special kinds change save/quit semantics.

blocks.title: What `'buftype'` actually picks
blocks.text:
Most buffers have `'buftype'` empty — they are tied to a file and `:w` writes to disk. The non-empty variants change that contract:

  • **`nofile`** — scratch buffer, never saved. Used by file explorers, fuzzy-finder UIs, scratch pads. `:w` is rejected.
  • **`acwrite`** — saving fires the `BufWriteCmd` autocmd instead of writing to disk; lets plugins implement *virtual* files (`fugitive://`, `oil://`, etc.).
  • **`quickfix`** — the quickfix or location list. Read-only listing of matches.
  • **`terminal`** — a job-controlled terminal pane. *Insert mode* types into the PTY, not the file.
  • **`prompt`** — a REPL-style buffer with a fixed prompt line and a per-Enter callback.
  • **`help`** — Vim's help buffers; read-only and tag-jumpable.
  • **`nowrite`** — like normal but `:w` is silently disallowed.

If you've ever typed in a buffer and gotten `E382: Cannot write, 'buftype' option is set` or `E21: Cannot make changes, 'modifiable' is off`, this table is what those messages are pointing at.
